## Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:

Temperature is linked to recruitment, spawning, and migration timing in tautog (Steimle and Shaheen 1999), but it is unclear how warming temperatures will affect phenology. Tautog exhibit some site fidelity, which may present a limited ability to expand or shift their range in response to warming temperatures (Langan et al. 2021). Moran and Gerry (2023) observed differences in prey type and feeding behavior in tautog exposed to varying temperatures, highlighting the need for further research on the relationship between increasing temperature and feeding behavior. Howell and Simpson (1994) reported that tautog are not tolerant of dissolved oxygen (DO) concentrations below 2 mg/l, and prefer DO levels above 3 mg/l. Tautog mortalities were reported by commercial fishers and were observed on the beach during a major anoxia-hypoxia episode off New Jersey in 1976. 

## Life History Synopsis:

Tautog are a slow-growing, long-lived coastal species inhabiting the outer coast of Nova Scotia to Georgia, although they are most abundant from Cape Cod to Chesapeake Bay (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). They do not reach maturity until age 3 (males) or 4 (females) and can live up to 25-30 years (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Tautog spawn in pairs, or in small groups with a single female and several males. The method depends on sex-ratios, the presence of a patriarchy, and availability of shelter and food (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). They spawn from April to August; occurring earlier (April) in the southern portion of the range. Peak spawning occurs in June/July in northern portions of habitat. They perform inshore/offshore migrations in order to spawn. In the north, tautog overwinter offshore and migrate inshore in the spring to spawn and feed before moving back offshore as temperatures decline (below 11°C) in the fall and winter. Southern populations may undertake shorter seasonal migrations or remain either inshore or offshore for the entire year (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Tautog enter a torpor state in waters between 7-10°C and prefer temperatures 10-22°C (Olla et al. 1974). In a laboratory setting, eggs hatch in 42-48 hours at 20-22°C with longer development times at lower temperatures (Perry 1994). Larval duration lasts about 20-25 days, depending on location (Sogard 1992). Larvae consume small fish and copepods, such as Acartia tonsa (Simpson 1989). Larvae survival is dependent on temperatures for both incubation period and recruitment. There was no direct evidence in the literature to support reliance on specific hydrographic conditions, however, spawning locations near the mouths of nursery habitats,and possible offshore advection suggest that local current patterns may play a role in larval distribution (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Juveniles prefer areas with high amounts of cover that they can hide in/around. However, they can utilize a variety of vertical habitats, including piers, artificial and real seagrass/algal beds, shells, and marine debris. Larger juvenile and adult tautog are closely associated with hard- surface, reef-like habitats, remaining close to their preferred home location (Sogard 1992). Juvenile tautog forage on benthic, as well as planktonic, prey. As they grow, juvenile tautog rely less on planktonic food resources and feed primarily on benthic prey, although the prey remain primarily crustaceans, such as decapods (Dorf 1994). Adult tautog are durophagous and feed chiefly on blue mussels and other shellfish throughout the year (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Seabirds, particularly cormorants, prey on juvenile tautog. Hakes, smooth and spiny dogfish, skates, and sea ravens have been reported to consume adult tautog (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Tautog is managed by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The Tautog Fishery Management Plan divides the stock into four regions: Massachusetts-Rhode Island (MARI), Long Island Sound (LIS), New Jersey-New York Bight (NJ-NYB), and Delaware-Maryland-Virginia (DMV) (ASMFC 2017). The 2025 stock assessment update indicated tautog are not overfished in MARI, LIS, or NJ-NYB, but overfished in DMV. Overfishing is not occurring in MARI or LIS, but the NJ-NYB and DMV regions are experiencing overfishing (ASMFC 2025).
